title: Hearing - Franchise Tax -  Margin (effective 01/01/2008) - 202512025H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-01902

CPA HEARING NO. 119,742

 

RE:       **************

TAXPAYER NO:     **************

AUDIT OFFICE:      **************

AUDIT PERIOD:     January 1, 2022 THROUGH December 31, 2022


Franchise Tax/RDT

 

 

SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-01904

CPA HEARING NO. 119,744

 

RE:       **************

TAXPAYER NO:     **************

AUDIT OFFICE:      **************

AUDIT PERIOD:     January 1, 2022 THROUGH December 31, 2022


Sales And Use Tax/RDT

 


SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-01905

CPA HEARING NO. 119,745

 

RE:       **************

TAXPAYER NO:     **************

AUDIT OFFICE:      **************

AUDIT PERIOD:     January 1, 2020 THROUGH December 31, 2022


Sales And Use Tax/RDT
 

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


TERESA GUTIERREZ

Representing Respondent


INDIVIDUAL A

Representing Petitioner

 

 

COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on December 29, 2025, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The results from this Decision are Attachments A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachments A reflect liabilities.[2]


The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.[3]  If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.


SIGNED on this 2nd day of December 2025
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachments A, Texas Notifications of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

ATTACHMENT B

 

 

State Office of Administrative Hearings

Kristofer S. Monson

Chief Administrative Law Judge

 

November 25, 2025

 

Augie Ruiz                                                             

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

INDIVIDUAL A                                                             

Petitioner

 

RE:    SOAH Docket Nos. 304-26-01902.13; 304-26-01903.26; 304-26-01904.26; 304-26-01905.26; 

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts Nos. 119,742; 119,743; 119,744; 119,745

 

Dear Parties:

 

Please be advised that Petitioner filed exceptions to the Proposal for Decision (PFD) in this matter on November 19, 2025. Staff filed a reply to Petitioner’s exceptions on November 21, 2025. Petitioner’s exceptions reassert arguments made in the hearing. Having reviewed the filings of the parties, and finding no errors in the findings of fact or conclusions of law, I recommend that the PFD be adopted as written. Because SOAH has concluded its involvement in the matter, the case is being remanded to the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts. See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.051(a).

 

Signed November 25, 2025.

 

ALJ Signature:

Keneshia Washington,

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 

ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket Nos. 304-26-01902; -01903;-01904;-01905                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing Nos. 119,742; 119,743; 119,744; 119,745

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) assessed ************** (Petitioner) for successor liability under Texas Tax Code §§ 111.020 and .024 for the franchise tax and sales and use tax liabilities of COMPANY A (Predecessor 1) and the sales and use tax liability of INDIVIDUAL B (Predecessor 2), owner of Predecessor 1. Petitioner requested redetermination, contending it was not intertwined with Predecessor 1 and did not complete a sale with Predecessor 2. In this Proposal for Decision (PFD), the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) finds Petitioner acquired Predecessors’ business through a fraudulent transfer or sham transaction and, therefore, the assessments should be affirmed.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested cases to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on September 29, 2025, issued Notices of Hearing by Written Submission to Petitioner. On September 30, 2025, ALJ Keneshia Washington issued an Order Consolidating Cases and Setting Written Submission Hearing, which consolidated the hearings for the purpose of issuing a single PFD and set the written submission hearing, filing deadlines, and record close date. Petitioner was represented by INDIVIDUAL A, a Managing Member of Petitioner. Teresa Gutierrez Smith represented Staff. The contested case record closed on November 17, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence Presented

 

Petitioner did not offer any evidence for the hearing. Staff submitted the pleadings the parties exchanged before the cases were referred to SOAH and offered the following exhibits for all four hearings:

 


	
	Secretary of State Formation Documents;
	
	
	Assumed Name Document;
	
	
	Certified Texas Tax Application;
	
	
	Secretary of State Formation Documents and Public Information Report;
	
	
	Certified AWM Enforcement Investigation Notes Interest Waiver;
	
	
	Copy of $5000.00 Check;
	
	
	Copy of Check with Petitioner’s Statement;
	
	
	Certified Public Information Report COMPANY C;
	
	
	Facebook Petitioner Posts;
	
	
	Certified Notice of Tax – 13;
	
	
	Certified CICS screenshot – 13;
	
	
	Certified Notice of Tax – 26 (missing – actual duplicate of Exhibit 10);
	
	
	Certified CICS screenshot;
	
	
	Certified Notice of Tax – 26 (missing – actual duplicate of Exhibit 10);
	
	
	Certified CICS screenshot;
	
	
	Certified CICS screenshot – 26;
	
	
	Certified Notice of Tax – 26 (missing – actual duplicate of Exhibit 10);
	
	
	Certified Hearing Grant Letter; and
	
	
	Certified Intro Letter.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits were admitted to the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff agreed to waive interest from April 1, 2024, through June 30, 2025.

 

C. Material Facts Established by the Evidence

 

Predecessor 1 filed a Certificate of Formation with the Texas Secretary of State on February 14, 2017, showing Predecessor 2 as a managing member. Predecessor 1 filed a 2021 Texas Franchise Tax Public Information Report showing Predecessor 2 as the sole owner and director. In a social media post published on November 16, 2021, Predecessor 2 tagged himself as #**************. On November 12, 2022, ************** published a social media post identifying its address as ADDRESS 1, CITY, Texas.

 

On December 16, 2022, Staff issued Predecessor 1 an estimated franchise tax jeopardy determination assessment for the period January 1, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determination went final on January 5, 2023. On January 13, 2023, Staff issued Predecessor 1 estimated sales and use tax jeopardy determination assessments for each quarterly period from December 1, 2020, through September 30, 2021, and January 1, 2022, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determinations went final on February 2, 2023. On January 13, 2023, Staff issued Predecessor 2 estimated sales and use tax jeopardy determination assessments for each quarterly period from January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determinations went final on February 2, 2023.

 

On January 25, 2023, Predecessor 1 published a social media post stating, “************** is now under new ownership and management! We are doing a lot of revamping please be patient as we get everything together to open up.” Petitioner filed a Certificate of Formation with the Texas Secretary of State on February 1, 2023, showing three managing members, including INDIVIDUAL A. On February 8, 2023, Petitioner filed an Assumed Name Certificate with the Texas Secretary of State indicating that Petitioner would be doing business as COMPANY B. Moreover, COMPANY C issued a 2022 Texas Franchise Tax Public Information Report showing INDIVIDUAL A as the president and sole director.

 

On February 9, 2023, during a call with a Comptroller Enforcement Officer, Predecessor 2 stated that he sold the business market name for $5,000. On March 3, 2023, during a call with a Comptroller Enforcement Officer, INDIVIDUAL A stated that she had been made aware of the assessments against Predecessors 1 and 2 and that she had not purchased Predecessors’ business. On March 7, 2023, during a follow up call, Ms. INDIVIDUAL A stated that she paid $5,000 to Predecessors 1 and 2 for the market name of the business but did not purchase any equipment or the food truck.

 

A notarized statement signed by Predecessor 2 and INDIVIDUAL A on March 16, 2023, contains the following statement “I, [Predecessor 2] hereby refunded the total amount on March 16 given to me from INDIVIDUAL A of COMPANY C as a Full Refund back to her for no interest in anything that belongs to me, including name inventory etc. She and her company is not held responsible for anything of my company or behalf of me. She is cancelling our deal 100% and will not be held liable for anything.” On March 16, 2023, Predecessor 2 remitted a cashier’s check to COMPANY C in the amount of $5,000.00.

 

On March 21, 2023, Petitioner filed a Sales Tax Permit Application establishing that Petitioner, with the above principal address, would operate a food truck as COMPANY B located at ADDRESS 2, CITY, Texas. INDIVIDUAL A signed as a member of Petitioner.

 

On April 3, 2023, Staff issued Petitioner four Texas Notices of Tax/Fee Due, including a notice for the franchise tax liability of Predecessor 1 for the period of January 1, through December 31, 2022, a notice for the sales and use tax liability of Predecessor 1 for the period of December 1, 2020, through September 30, 2021, a notice for the sales and use tax liability for Predecessor 1 for the period of January 1, through December 31, 2022, and a notice for the sales and use tax liability for Predecessor 2 for the period of January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022. The notices were issued under Texas Tax Code Sections 111.020 and/or 111.024, and 111.022 due to the acquisition of Predecessor. The amount of tax assessed against Petitioner was based on the above listed jeopardy determinations against Predecessors 1 and 2 that had gone final and remained unpaid.

 

Petitioner requested redetermination, contending it and Predecessors have no affiliation and that it did not purchase a business from the Predecessors. Staff contends the evidence supports a finding that Petitioner purchased the business of Predecessors 1 and 2 for consideration or that the business was transferred through a fraudulent transfer or sham transaction with the intent to evade, hinder, delay, or prevent the collection of an outstanding tax liability. Therefore, Staff argues, Petitioner is liable for the business’s tax debts.

 

D. ALJ’s Analysis and Recommendations

 


	
	Texas Tax Code § 111.020
	


 

Texas Tax Code § 111.020 provides that if a person who is liable for an amount under Title 2 sells the business or the stock of goods of the business or quits the business, then the successor to the seller shall withhold an amount from the purchase price sufficient to pay the amount due, unless the seller first obtains a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the amount has been paid or a certificate stating that no amount is due. If the purchaser of the business or stock of goods fails to do so, the purchaser is liable for the delinquent taxes to the extent of the value of the purchase price.

 

Texas Tax Code § 111.020 is an imposition statute. Therefore, in a successor liability redetermination hearing, Staff bears the initial burden to establish, prima facie, that imposition of successor liability is appropriate. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,836 (2014). A prima facie case is one that allows a fact finder to infer the fact at issue and rule in the party’s favor. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 104,533 (2012). Staff must show that Predecessor sold its business or stock of goods to Petitioner, that Predecessor was liable for a sales and use tax deficiency, and that its liability was final and unpaid at the time Petitioner was assessed as a successor. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 109,500 (2014), 106,871 (2013).

 

When determining if a business has been or will be sold, the Comptroller will examine the transaction to determine what the parties to the transaction intended to buy and sell. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d). The answer in each situation will depend on the type of business involved. Id.

 

A seller may have sold a business even when few assets were transferred. Depending on the type of business involved, a business may be sold if an owner sells:

 


	
	a building, land, furniture, fixtures, inventory, and the right to use the seller’s trade name; or
	
	
	all the capital assets of a business; or
	
	
	the name and goodwill of a business; or
	
	
	all the inventory of a business; or
	
	
	fixed assets and realty necessary to operate a similar business as the seller at the same location. Id.
	


 

In the present case, Predecessor 2 operated Predecessor 1 as a food truck under the name of ************** located at ADDRESS 1, CITY, Texas. Approximately two weeks after the tax determinations had been issued, Predecessor 1 made a social media post confirming an upcoming change in management. About a month later, Petitioner filed a Certificate of Formation with the Texas Secretary of State followed by a notification of assumed name indicating that Petitioner would be doing business as COMPANY B. By about a month later, Predecessor 2 and Petitioner confirmed to Comptroller Enforcement personnel that Petitioner purchased the market name of Predecessor 1 for $5,000. Per a notarized statement and a cashier’s check, the $5,000 in consideration was returned to Petitioner. Approximately one week later, Petitioner filed a sales tax permit application to operate COMPANY B to be operated at ADDRESS 2, CITY, Texas.

 

The ALJ finds that there is sufficient evidence to prove, prima facie, that Petitioner acquired the right to use the trade name and goodwill of Predecessor 1, an entity with outstanding tax liabilities. However, based on the plain language of Section 111.020, applicability of the statute requires a sale of the business for consideration. Because record evidence demonstrates that consideration for the business transfer was returned to Petitioner and Petitioner proceeded with preparation to operate the business, there is insufficient evidence for Staff to meet its prima facie burden to establish Petitioner’s liability as a successor under Texas Tax Code § 111.020.

 

2. Texas Tax Code § 111.024

 

Staff also contends Petitioner acquired the business or the assets of the business through a fraudulent transfer or sham transaction. Texas Tax Code § 111.024(a) provides that a person who acquires a business or the assets of a business from a taxpayer through a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction is liable for any tax, penalty, and interest owed by the taxpayer. Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b) provides that a transfer of a business or the assets of a business is considered a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction if the taxpayer made the transfer or undertook the transaction:

 


	
	with intent to evade, hinder, delay, or prevent the collection of any tax, penalty, or interest owed under this title; or
	
	
	without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the business or business assets subject to the transfer or transaction.
	


 

Staff has a prima facie burden to establish that Petitioner falls within the category of taxpayers the Legislature intended to tax in a Texas Tax Code § 111.024 assessment. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 44,886 and 44,890 (2005). If Staff meets its burden, then Petitioner is required to show, by a preponderance of evidence, that the assessment is incorrect. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 101,355 (2013), 104,681 (2012), 100,503 (2009); see also 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).

 

As set out above, consideration for the market name and goodwill of Predecessor 1 was returned to Petitioner, and Petitioner proceeded to prepare to operate the business without giving any form of consideration to Predecessors 1 or 2. However, under Texas Tax Code § 111.024, it is the acquisition of a business, and not strictly the purchase of a business, that is considered. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,532 (2021).

 

The ALJ finds that Staff’s evidence demonstrates that Petitioner acquired the business of Predecessors 1 and 2 without the conveyance of a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the business or business assets. Therefore, the ALJ concludes Petitioner acquired the business of Predecessors 1 and 2 through a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction. See Tex. Tax Code § 111.024(b)(2). Petitioner failed to demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that it did not acquire the business or that Predecessor 1 and 2 received a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the Predecessors’ business or business assets. The assessment of successor liability against Petitioner under Texas Tax Code § 111.024 should be upheld.

 

Texas Tax Code § 111.024(c) includes several factors to determine whether Petitioner acquired Predecessor’s business with intent to evade, hinder, delay, or prevent the collection of any tax, penalty, or interest owed under Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1). Based on the facts described above, Predecessor 2 had already been given notice of the tax jeopardy determinations against himself and Predecessor 1 when he accepted consideration for use of the market name for the business and good will from Petitioner and publicly notified the customer base about the upcoming change in management. After Petitioner became aware of the tax liabilities at issue and discussing the matter with Comptroller Enforcement personnel, Predecessor 2 and Petitioner transferred consideration and signed documentation, indicative of efforts to prevent impeding collection action by the Comptroller against Petitioner. After documentation and communications to the Comptroller Enforcement personnel to assert that Petitioner would not proceed with operation of the business, Petitioner prepared to begin operating a food truck on the same street with a very similar name, supporting an inference of efforts to conceal the fact that the business had been transferred to Petitioner and would proceed operating.

 

Hence, several factors under Texas Tax Code § 111.024(c) support the conclusion that Petitioner acquired the business of Predecessors 1 and 2 with intent to evade tax and the applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1). However, the applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1) is moot given the applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(2).

 

3. Interest

 

Petitioner requested a waiver interest. Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c). The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Id. § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014). Staff agrees to waive interest for the time period April 1, 2024, through June 30, 2025. Petitioner did not demonstrate any of the factors that warrant additional interest waiver. The contention should be denied.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	COMPANY A (Predecessor 1) filed a Certificate of Formation with the Texas Secretary of State on February 14, 2017, showing INDIVIDUAL B (Predecessor 2) as a managing member.
	
	
	Predecessor 1 filed a 2021 Texas Franchise Tax Public Information Report showing Predecessor 2 as the sole owner and director.
	
	
	In a social media post published on November 16, 2021, Predecessor 2 tagged himself as #**************. On November 12, 2022, ************** published a social media post identifying its address as ADDRESS 1, CITY, Texas.
	
	
	On December 16, 2022, the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) issued Predecessor 1 an estimated franchise tax jeopardy determination assessment for the period January 1, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determination went final on January 5, 2023.
	
	
	On January 13, 2023, Staff issued Predecessor 1 estimated sales and use tax jeopardy determination assessments for each quarterly period from December 1, 2020, through September 30, 2021, and January 1, 2022, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determinations went final on February 2, 2023.
	
	
	On January 13, 2023, Staff issued Predecessor 2 estimated sales and use tax jeopardy determination assessments for each quarterly period from January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022, along with applicable penalties and interest. The determinations went final on February 2, 2023.
	
	
	On January 25, 2023, Predecessor 1 published a social media post stating, “************** is now under new ownership and management! We are doing a lot of revamping please be patient as we get everything together to open up.”
	
	
	Petitioner filed a Certificate of Formation with the Texas Secretary of State on February 1, 2023, showing three managing members, including INDIVIDUAL A. On February 8, 2023, Petitioner filed an Assumed Name Certificate with the Texas Secretary of State indicating that Petitioner would be doing business as COMPANY B.
	
	
	COMPANY C issued a 2022 Texas Franchise Tax Public Information Report showing INDIVIDUAL A as the president and sole director.
	
	
	On February 9, 2023, during a call with a Comptroller Enforcement Officer, Predecessor 2 stated that he sold the business market name for $5,000. On March 3, 2023, during a call with a Comptroller Enforcement Officer, INDIVIDUAL A stated that she had been made aware of the assessments against Predecessors 1 and 2 and that she had not purchased Predecessors’ business.
	
	
	On March 7, 2023, during a follow up call, Ms. INDIVIDUAL A stated that she paid $5,000 to Predecessors 1 and 2 for the market name of the business but did not purchase any equipment or the food truck.
	
	
	A notarized statement signed by Predecessor 2 and INDIVIDUAL A on March 16, 2023, contains the following statement “I, [Predecessor 2] hereby refunded the total amount on March 16 given to me from INDIVIDUAL A of COMPANY C as a Full Refund back to her for no interest in anything that belongs to me, including name inventory etc. She and her company is not held responsible for anything of my company or behalf of me. She is cancelling our deal 100% and will not be held liable for anything.”
	
	
	On March 16, 2023, Predecessor 2 remitted a cashier’s check to COMPANY C in the amount of $5,000.00.
	
	
	On March 21, 2023, Petitioner filed a Sales Tax Permit Application establishing that Petitioner, with the above principal address, would operate a food truck as COMPANY B located at ADDRESS 2, CITY, Texas. INDIVIDUAL A signed as a member of Petitioner.
	
	
	On April 3, 2023, Staff issued Petitioner four Texas Notices of Tax/Fee Due, including a notice for the franchise tax liability of Predecessor 1 for the period of January 1, through December 31, 2022, a notice for the sales and use tax liability of Predecessor 1 for the period of December 1, 2020, through September 30, 2021, a notice for the sales and use tax liability for Predecessor 1 for the period of January 1, through December 31, 2022, and a notice for the sales and use tax liability for Predecessor 2 for the period of January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022.
	
	
	The notices were issued under Texas Tax Code Sections 111.020 and/or 111.024, and 111.022 due to the acquisition of Predecessor. The amount of tax assessed against Petitioner was based on the above listed jeopardy determinations against Predecessors 1 and 2 that had gone final and remained unpaid.
	
	
	Petitioner timely requested redeterminations.
	
	
	Staff referred the above-referenced case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On September 29, 2025, Staff issued Petitioner Notices of Hearing by Written Submission. The notices contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 30, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington issued an Order Consolidating Cases and Setting Written Submission Hearing, which consolidated the hearings for the purpose of issuing a single PFD and set the written submission hearing, filing deadlines, and record close date.
	
	
	Petitioner did not submit any evidence for the written submission hearing.
	
	
	The record closed on November 17, 2025.
	
	
	Staff agreed to waive interest from April 1, 2024, through June 30, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over these matters. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided a proper and timely notice of hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	If a person who is liable for an amount under Title 2 sells the business or the stock of goods of the business or quits the business, then the successor to the seller shall withhold an amount from the purchase price sufficient to pay the amount due, unless the seller first obtains a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the amount has been paid or a certificate stating that no amount is due. If the purchaser of the business or stock of goods fails to do so, the purchaser is liable for the delinquent taxes to the extent of the value of the purchase price. Texas Tax Code § 111.020.
	
	
	Texas Tax Code § 111.020 is an imposition statute.
	
	
	In a successor liability redetermination hearing, Staff bears the initial burden to establish, prima facie, that imposition of successor liability is appropriate. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,836 (2014).
	
	
	A prima facie case is one that allows a fact finder to infer the fact at issue and rule in the party’s favor. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 104,533 (2012).
	
	
	Staff must show that Predecessor sold its business or stock of goods to Petitioner, that Predecessor was liable for a sales and use tax deficiency, and that its liability was final and unpaid at the time Petitioner was assessed as a successor. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 109,500 (2014), 106,871 (2013).
	
	
	When determining if a business has been or will be sold, the Comptroller will examine the transaction to determine what the parties to the transaction intended to buy and sell. The answer in each situation will depend on the type of business involved. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).
	
	
	A seller may have sold a business even when few assets were transferred. Depending on the type of business involved, a business may be sold if an owner sells: a building, land, furniture, fixtures, inventory, and the right to use the seller’s trade name; all the capital assets of a business; the name and goodwill of a business; all the inventory of a business; or fixed assets and realty necessary to operate a similar business as the seller at the same location. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).
	
	
	There is sufficient evidence to prove, prima facie, that Petitioner acquired the right to use the trade name and goodwill of Predecessor 1, an entity with outstanding tax liabilities.
	
	
	Because record evidence demonstrates that consideration for the business transfer was returned to Petitioner and Petitioner proceeded with preparation to operate the business, there is insufficient evidence for Staff to meet its prima facie burden to establish Petitioner’s liability as a successor under Texas Tax Code § 111.020.
	
	
	A person who acquires a business or the assets of a business from a taxpayer through a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction is liable for any tax, penalty, and interest owed by the taxpayer. Texas Tax Code § 111.024(a).
	
	
	A transfer of a business or the assets of a business is considered a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction if the taxpayer made the transfer or undertook the transaction: with intent to evade, hinder, delay, or prevent the collection of any tax, penalty, or interest owed under this title; or without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the business or business assets subject to the transfer or transaction. Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b).
	
	
	Staff has a prima facie burden to establish that Petitioner falls within the category of taxpayers the Legislature intended to tax in a Texas Tax Code § 111.024 assessment. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 44,886 and 44,890 (2005).
	
	
	If Staff meets its burden, then Petitioner is required to show, by a preponderance of evidence, that the assessment is incorrect. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 101,355 (2013), 104,681 (2012), 100,503 (2009); see also 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	Under Texas Tax Code § 111.024, it is the acquisition of a business, and not strictly the purchase of a business, that is considered. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,532 (2021).
	
	
	Staff’s evidence demonstrates that Petitioner acquired the business of Predecessors 1 and 2, and the Predecessors did not receive a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the business or business assets.
	
	
	Petitioner acquired the business of Predecessors 1 and 2 through a fraudulent transfer or a sham transaction. See Tex. Tax Code § 111.024(b)(2).
	
	
	Petitioner failed to demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that it did not acquire the business of Predecessor 1 and 2 or that Predecessors 1 or 2 received a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the business or business assets.
	
	
	The assessment of successor liability against Petitioner under Texas Tax Code § 111.024 should be upheld.
	
	
	Texas Tax Code § 111.024(c) includes several factors to determine whether Petitioner acquired Predecessor’s business with intent to evade, hinder, delay, or prevent the collection of any tax, penalty, or interest owed under Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1).
	
	
	Several factors under Texas Tax Code § 111.024(c) support the conclusion that Petitioner acquired Predecessor with intent to evade tax and the applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1).
	
	
	The applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(1) is moot given the applicability of Texas Tax Code § 111.024(b)(2).
	
	
	Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c).
	
	
	The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e).
	
	
	To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014).
	
	
	Petitioner did not demonstrate any of the factors that warrant additional interest waiver, so the contention should be denied.
	


 

Signed November 19, 2025.

 

ALJ Signature:

Keneshia Washington

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 

ENDNOTES


[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf

[2] At present, insufficient information is available to determine which items and amounts are disputed or undisputed for purposes of Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112. In the absence of this information, the Comptroller will assume the entire amount of the assessment, as it appears in Comptroller’s Decision Attachment A, the Notification of Hearing Results, remains in dispute.

If Petitioner intends to sue the comptroller to dispute an amount of tax, penalty, or interest assessed in a deficiency redetermination or jeopardy determination under Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 111, Petitioner is required to file a motion for rehearing that “states the specific grounds of error and the disputed amounts associated with the grounds of error.” Tex. Tax Code § 112.201(a)(3). Petitioner should refer to Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112, for further guidance regarding a suit after redetermination.

[3] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c).
